content.problemStatement: Write a C program that takes an integer as input and prints its reverse. Use a while loop for the reversal process.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the integer obtained by reversing the decimal digits of N while preserving its sign, each on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 10001 is a palindrome, so its reverse is the same.

solution.approach: We process each test case independently. For a given integer N we keep its sign, work with its absolute value and repeatedly extract the last digit using modulo 10, appending it to a result variable (rev = rev*10 + digit). The loop stops when the number becomes zero. Finally we re‑apply the original sign and output the result. This uses only O(1) extra memory and runs in time proportional to the number of decimal digits of N, which is at most 7 for the given constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(D) per test case, where D is the number of digits of N (≤7). Overall O(T·D).

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int T=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(int i=0;i<T;i++){String line;while((line=br.readLine())!=null&&line.trim().isEmpty()){}long num=Long.parseLong(line.trim());long sign=num<0?-1:1;num=Math.abs(num);long rev=0;while(num!=0){rev=rev*10+num%10;num/=10;}sb.append(sign*rev);if(i!=T-1)sb.append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
